origin: Southwestern & Eastern Cape Province, South Africa
habitat: Grassy mountain slopes and marshy stream margins of the Cape
blurb: The original torch lily: a bold clump of arching strap foliage that sends up tall spikes graduating from blazing red-orange at the top to lemon-yellow at the base — a hummingbird and bee magnet from midsummer into fall. Tough and xeric-once-established, but sharply drained soil is non-negotiable.
size: 3–5 ft tall in bloom × 2–3 ft wide (foliage clump 18–24 in)
water: Moderate; sharp drainage critical — wet winter crowns rot
seasons: Bold evergreen strap foliage year-round (may bronze at tips in hard winters); red-to-yellow torch spikes midsummer into fall; no ornamental winter structure once leaves lie flat
wildlife: Hummingbirds and bees work the nectar-rich tubular flowers
toxic: Not significantly toxic; no part is eaten — grown as an ornamental

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zones 5–9; most sources rate K. uvaria at zone 5a, which covers the Front Range (Boulder is 5b–6a).[1][2] In practice this plant sits on the borderline here: given excellent drainage and the leaf-tying winter trick (see Maintenance), established clumps survive most Front Range winters without issue, but a combination of wet clay, standing water around the crown, and a bitter open winter can kill it. Sharp drainage is the single biggest hardiness factor — a dry crown survives cold; a wet crown does not.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (April–May) after danger of hard frost, or in early fall (August–September) to give roots time to anchor before freeze. Site selection is everything: choose the sunniest, best-drained spot you have. Boulder's heavy alkaline clay must be amended with coarse grit and a generous helping of compost worked 12 in. deep, or build a raised bed or berm so the crown sits above any wet zone.[1][2] In containers, use a gritty, fast-draining mix; container plants bloom reliably and can be moved to a sheltered spot for winter, but container roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground roots — overwinter the pot in an unheated garage or shed if temperatures will drop below 10°F for extended periods.[2]
care.sun: Full sun is non-negotiable — aim for 6 or more hours of direct sun daily. Afternoon shade reduces flower count and weakens stems. On the Front Range the intense high-altitude sun suits this South African native perfectly; just ensure moisture is adequate to match the light intensity.[1][2]
care.soil: The number-one cultural requirement: sharply drained soil. Crown and root rot in wet, heavy ground is the primary way red hot poker fails on the Front Range.[1][2] Native to Cape mountain slopes and grassland edges, it is built for lean, fast-draining conditions. Amend Boulder's clay heavily with coarse sand or horticultural grit before planting, or plant on a slope, raised bed, or berm. A pH of 6.0–7.5 suits it (slightly acidic to neutral),[2] though it tolerates the alkaline side of our native soils as long as drainage is good. Avoid rich, soggy border soil — it promotes soft, floppy growth and crown disease.
care.water: Once established, moderate and drought-tolerant: a deep soak every week to ten days through summer heat is plenty; cut to once every two weeks in cooler stretches and let rain cover it in spring and fall.[2] Newly planted divisions need regular watering through their first season to establish. The critical rule: keep moisture away from the crown, especially in fall and winter — wet winter crowns rot.[1][2] Drip irrigation at the soil line is safer than overhead watering that soaks the foliar canopy.
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in. apart for compact cultivars, or 2–3 ft for the full K. uvaria species form; each clump spreads slowly via rhizomes to about 2–3 ft wide over several years.[1][2] Plant in groups of three or more for the boldest effect — a single clump can look spindly while a drift is dramatic.
care.propagation: Division in spring (April–May) is the preferred method and the way to maintain clumps.[1][2] Dig and lift the entire clump once growth resumes, slice through the rootball with a sharp spade (avoid sawing — it shreds rhizomes), and ensure each section has viable growing tips at the crown. Replant divisions immediately in amended, gritty soil at the original depth; water in well. Divide every 3–4 years to keep clumps vigorous — old crowns get crowded and produce fewer spikes. From seed: surface-sow fresh seed in spring on a gritty, barely moist mix; germination is variable, seedlings take 2–3 years to first bloom, and named cultivars don't come true from seed.[2]
care.bloom: Tall spikes of densely packed tubular flowers emerge midsummer (July) and continue into early fall (September–October) on well-established clumps.[1][2] The classic bicolor runs red-orange at the top (newly opened buds) graduating to yellow at the base (spent flowers), giving each spike a torch-like gradient. Deadhead spent spikes by cutting them cleanly at the base — this keeps the clump tidy, prevents unwanted self-seeding, and encourages reblooming.[1][2] Hummingbirds and bees work the tubular flowers vigorously; the hummingbird traffic alone makes this one of the best late-season pollinator plants on the Front Range.
care.feeding: Light to moderate feeder. A 2 in. side-dressing of compost worked in around the base in spring is usually sufficient; over-rich feeding produces floppy stems and soft growth prone to rot.[2] If the soil is very lean, an optional application of slow-release balanced fertilizer (5-5-5 or similar) in early spring is fine. Avoid heavy nitrogen feeds that push vegetative growth at the expense of flowers.
care.maintenance: The critical Front Range maintenance step is winter crown protection. In autumn, before hard frost, gather the long arching leaves and tie them loosely upright in a bundle over the crown — this forms a natural waterproof canopy that sheds winter rain and snow-melt away from the base and provides a few degrees of insulation.[1] Do NOT cut the foliage back hard in fall: the leaves are the insulation. A ring of dry mulch (straw, shredded bark) around (not over) the crown adds further protection in zone 5 winters. Tidy up in spring (April) once growth resumes — cut away the winter-damaged leaf tips and any dead outer foliage to the base. Remove spent flower spikes cleanly at the base throughout the bloom season.[1][2]
care.troubles: Crown and root rot in wet, heavy, or poorly drained soil is the dominant failure mode — drainage is everything.[1][2] Thrips occasionally infest flowers and foliage (look for silvery stippling and distorted buds); insecticidal soap or neem oil controls them.[1] Slugs and snails can shred emerging new foliage in spring; manage with iron phosphate bait. The plant is generally resistant to deer and rabbits. On the Front Range the main killers are: (1) siting in poorly-drained clay — fix at planting time, not after; (2) skipping the autumn leaf-tie, letting crown-freeze and crown-rot take hold; (3) dividing too late in fall when roots have no time to re-establish before freeze.
care.companions: Pair with other bold, sun-loving, drought-tolerant plants that echo its vertical drama — ornamental grasses (little bluestem, blue oat grass), knautia, blanket flower, and Russian sage make excellent partners. The bicolor red-to-yellow spikes read well against the silver-blue of salvia or the golden discs of rudbeckia. Plant near hummingbird-attracting companions (crocosmia, penstemon, monarda) to build a pollinator-rich midsummer border. Avoid pairing with moisture-loving plants that would require wet soil the poker can't tolerate.

edible.summary: Red hot poker is a garden ornamental with no food use and no significant toxicity — not eaten, not poisonous.[1][2]
edible.caution: Kniphofia uvaria has no documented edible use; no part of the plant is eaten as food. Plants for a Future rates its edibility as 0 with 'None known' under edible uses.[3] No significant mammalian toxicity is attributed to it in the horticultural literature, and it is not a recognized poisonous plant.[1] The milky sap of the stems can mildly irritate skin or eyes — avoid rubbing your face after handling and wash hands after working with the cut stems.[2] Treat it as an ornamental only and do not consume any part.
